title: Not one AI - a zoo
content: The word 'AI' hides a whole zoo of different model types, sorted by what they take in and what they produce. LLMs read and write text. Vision models understand images. Speech models transcribe and speak. Image and video generators create pictures and film from descriptions. Different species, different jobs.
next: The newest species can do several of these at once.

title: Multimodal: one model, many senses
content: A multimodal model handles several formats in one brain: show it a photo of your fridge and ask, in text, what to cook - it reads the image and writes the recipe. Like a colleague who can read, look, and listen instead of one who only answers email. Frontier models from the major labs are increasingly multimodal by default.
next: So who actually builds these things?

title: The major providers
content: Six names cover most of the news. OpenAI builds the GPT models behind ChatGPT. Anthropic builds Claude. Google DeepMind builds Gemini. Meta builds Llama. Mistral AI is Europe's flagship lab. xAI builds Grok. Know these six and the model names attached to them, and most AI headlines instantly make sense.
next: One dividing line splits this list in a really important way.

title: Open weights vs closed
content: Some models are closed: you can only reach them through the provider's API, and the internals stay secret - GPT and Claude work this way. Others publish their weights (the trained parameters) for anyone to download and run on their own machines - Meta's Llama is the famous example. Roughly: a secret family recipe you can order at the restaurant, versus a recipe printed for anyone to cook at home.
next: With all these options - how does anyone choose?

title: The triangle: capability, speed, cost
content: Every model choice trades off three things. Bigger models are more capable but slower and pricier per token; smaller ones are fast and cheap but less brilliant. That is why providers ship families in sizes - like Claude's Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku. You would not book a moving truck to deliver a letter: routine tasks get small models, hard reasoning gets big ones.
next: But how do you know which model is 'best' at something?

title: Benchmarks: useful, gameable
content: Benchmarks are standardized tests - math problems, coding tasks, exam questions - used to compare models, and leaderboards rank the results. They are genuinely useful but easy to over-trust: models can be tuned toward popular tests, like judging chefs solely by one signature dish. The scores narrow your shortlist; trying a model on your own task picks the winner.
next: One warning before you memorize any of these names.

title: Names expire, concepts do not
content: Model names and leaderboard rankings churn every few months - whatever is 'best' today will be old news within a year. But everything else you have learned holds steady: tokens, context, prompts, tools, RAG, memory, workflows, agents describe how the pieces fit, regardless of whose model sits in the middle. Learn the map, not the traffic.
next: Which brings us to the end of the journey - and the whole stack at once.

title: You now speak AI
content: The full stack, top to bottom: a model predicts tokens; prompts and context steer it; tools give it hands; RAG gives it your knowledge; memory layers make it personal; APIs make it rentable; workflows and agents put it to work; MCP plugs it all together. Next time someone drops a buzzword - hallucination, fine-tuning, vector database, agent - you know exactly which shelf it sits on.
next: That is the whole landscape. The hype will keep changing - your map will not.
